3.happening: Measure the grid — how many rows it has.
3.why: We need the bounds to scan every cell and to test neighbors.
3.invariant: The grid's dimensions are fixed for the whole run.

4.happening: Measure how many columns each row has.
4.why: Column count bounds our left/right moves.
4.invariant: Every row has the same width.

5.why: We must remember which cells we've reached so no island is counted twice.

6.happening: Loop over each row to give it a fresh tracker row.
6.why: state must mirror the grid's shape.
6.invariant: Each built row holds exactly {cols} zeros.

7.happening: Add a row of zeros — every cell starts unseen (0).
7.invariant: No cell is marked before the search begins.

8.why: We haven't found any islands yet.
8.invariant: count equals islands fully discovered so far.

9.happening: Reset the current-row marker to -1 (no active cell).
9.why: There's an active cell only while flooding an island.

10.why: Same — no cell is being processed yet.
10.invariant: cur_r and cur_c together locate the active cell.

11.happening: List the four orthogonal moves (up, down, right, left).
11.why: Islands connect only horizontally or vertically, never diagonally.
11.invariant: Only these four directions are ever tried.

12.happening: Scan rows from top to bottom looking for land.
12.why: Every cell must be checked to find all islands.
12.invariant: Rows above sr are fully scanned.

13.happening: Scan columns left to right within row {sr}.
13.why: We inspect each cell exactly once.
13.invariant: Cells left of sc in this row are already handled.

14.happening: Cell ({sr},{sc}) is land we haven't reached — a new island begins here.
14.why: Any unvisited land cell must belong to a brand-new island.
14.invariant: Every island is discovered from its first-scanned cell.

14.happening: Cell ({sr},{sc}) is water or already flooded — skip it.
14.why: Water starts no island, and flooded land is already counted.
14.invariant: We never count the same island twice.

15.happening: Found untouched land at ({sr},{sc}) — count a new island.
15.why: Each unvisited land cell starts exactly one island.
15.invariant: count is about to rise to include this island.

16.happening: Make an empty queue for this island's flood.
16.why: The queue holds discovered cells waiting their turn, first-in first-out.
16.invariant: Breadth-first: we explore in the order cells were found.

17.happening: Enqueue the seed cell ({sr},{sc}) at the back.
17.invariant: The queue holds this island's frontier in discovery order.

18.happening: Mark the seed cell as seen (1).
18.why: So it can never be enqueued again.
18.invariant: A cell enters the queue at most once.

19.happening: Set the front marker head to 0.
19.why: It points at the next cell to process.
19.invariant: Cells before head are already processed.

20.happening: The queue has {len(queue)} cell(s), front at {head} — keep flooding.
20.why: Cells discovered but not yet processed remain.
20.invariant: head only moves toward the back as the island fills.

20.happening: The front caught up to the back — this island is completely flooded.
20.invariant: The whole connected component is now marked done.

21.happening: Read the cell at the front of the queue.
21.why: Breadth-first processes the earliest-discovered cell next.

22.why: This cell is now being processed, so move the front forward.

23.why: We need its coordinates to reach neighbors.

24.why: Row and column together locate the active cell.
24.invariant: cur_r, cur_c now point at the cell being flooded.

25.happening: Mark the active cell ({cur_r},{cur_c}) fully done (2).
25.why: It now firmly belongs to the current island.

26.happening: Try each of the four directions from this cell.
26.why: To find land connected to it.
26.invariant: All four neighbors are examined before moving on.

27.why: The direction's row offset added to the active cell.
27.invariant: nr may be off-grid — we check next.

28.why: The direction's column offset added to the active cell.
28.invariant: nc may be off-grid — we check next.

29.when: nr >= 0 && nr < rows && nc >= 0 && nc < cols && grid[nr][nc] == '1' && state[nr][nc] == 0
29.happening: Neighbor ({nr},{nc}) is fresh land — add it to the frontier.
29.why: It's in-bounds, is land, and hasn't been seen.
29.invariant: Only new land ever joins the queue.

29.happening: Neighbor ({nr},{nc}) is off-grid, water, or already seen — ignore it.
29.why: Only in-bounds unseen land can extend the island.
29.invariant: Out-of-range and visited cells are never re-added.

30.happening: Mark the new land neighbor ({nr},{nc}) as seen (1).
30.why: Claim it before enqueuing so it can't be queued twice.
30.invariant: Seen cells are waiting in the queue.

31.happening: Enqueue the neighbor at the back.
31.why: It'll be processed after everything discovered before it — the wavefront expands evenly.
31.invariant: The island grows in rings of equal distance.

32.happening: Island fully flooded — clear the current-row marker.
32.why: No active cell while we resume scanning.
32.invariant: cur_r back to -1 hides the cursor.

33.why: The cursor is fully hidden until the next island.
33.invariant: The next land cell found starts a new flood.

34.why: Every cell has been scanned and every island flooded.

init.happening: Setting up: measuring the grid and preparing an empty visited-tracker.
init.why: We need dimensions and a way to remember reached cells before searching.

loop.happening: Scanning the grid for the next piece of unvisited land.
loop.why: Each unvisited land cell reveals a new island.

check.happening: Deciding whether this cell starts or extends an island.

update.happening: Recording progress — counting an island or marking a cell.
update.why: State must reflect what we've discovered.

move.happening: Advancing the wavefront — dequeuing a cell and reaching toward neighbors.
move.why: Breadth-first exploration expands the island in rings.

return.happening: Search complete — reporting the island count: {count}.
return.why: All cells scanned, all islands flooded.
